questions.q: Which vital sign measures the number of breaths a patient takes per minute?
What is the primary purpose of wound care in nursing?
In which healthcare setting do nurses typically provide emergency medical care and monitor critically ill patients?
Which blood type is considered the universal recipient?
What is the main responsibility of nurses when preparing a patient for surgical procedures?
choices: Preparing the sterile field and supporting the surgical team

questions.q: Which vital sign indicates the force of blood pushing against artery walls?
How do nurses contribute to managing high blood pressure in patients?
choices: By administering antihypertensive medications and educating patients on lifestyle changes
By refusing to monitor vital signs

questions.q: What is the primary role of nurses in monitoring respiratory function?
choices: Assessing breathing patterns and oxygen levels

questions.q: Which nursing action is essential during a blood transfusion?
choices: Ensuring correct blood type matching and monitoring for adverse reactions

questions.q: What is a primary duty of nurses in patient nutrition management?
choices: Managing nutrition intake and administering relevant medications

questions.q: How do nurses assist in preventing hospital-acquired infections?
choices: By practicing proper hand hygiene and following sterile technique

questions.q: Which of the following is a core responsibility of nurses in hospital settings?
choices: Regularly monitoring and recording patient vital signs

questions.q: What educational requirement typically applies to registered nurses in most developed countries?
choices: Completion of a nursing program and passing licensing examinations

questions.q: Which specialty involves nurses providing care specific to pregnant women and newborns?
What is the primary focus of palliative care nursing?
choices: Providing comfort and improving quality of life for patients with serious illnesses

questions.q: What is a key aspect of nurse-physician collaboration in patient care?
choices: Nurses making independent diagnoses without physician input
Nurses refusing to communicate with physicians
Nurses and physicians working together to develop comprehensive treatment plans

questions.q: In which healthcare setting do nurses typically provide long-term care and support for elderly patients?
choices: Nursing home or assisted living facility

questions.q: What is the primary goal of patient education provided by nurses?
choices: To empower patients with knowledge for self-care and disease prevention

questions.q: What is the primary role of nurses in patient care?
choices: Providing direct care and monitoring patient health

questions.q: Which historical figure is credited with founding modern nursing?
What blood type is considered the universal donor?
In the nursing process, which step involves collecting information about a patient's health status?
Which nursing specialty focuses on caring for patients before, during, and after surgery?
What is the primary concern when administering a blood transfusion to a patient?
choices: Warming the blood to room temperature

questions.q: Which of the following is a core responsibility of nurses in public health settings?
What is the normal resting heart rate range for a healthy adult?
Which body system do nurses primarily monitor by assessing blood pressure and pulse?
What is a primary nursing intervention for patients with open wounds?
choices: Wound cleaning and dressing to prevent infection

questions.q: In what year did Florence Nightingale establish the first nursing school?
What is the primary function of white blood cells?
choices: Fighting infections and supporting immune response

questions.q: How do nurses typically assess a patient's respiratory function?
choices: By observing breathing patterns and listening to lung sounds

questions.q: What is the term for the general approach nurses use to organize and deliver patient care?
Which of the following best describes the role of nurses in collaborating with physicians?
choices: Providing patient data and assessments to support treatment decisions

questions.q: What is the normal body temperature range for a healthy adult?
Which nursing intervention is appropriate for managing a patient's nutritional needs?
choices: Assessing dietary intake and coordinating appropriate nutrition support

questions.q: What is the primary purpose of patient monitoring in cardiac care nursing?
choices: Assessing heart function and detecting changes in cardiac status

questions.q: What is the primary vital sign that measures the number of times a person's heart beats in one minute?
What is the medical procedure called when blood from one person is transferred into another person's bloodstream?
What is one of the primary responsibilities of a registered nurse in patient care?
choices: Administering medications and monitoring patient health

questions.q: Which type of white blood cells are primarily responsible for fighting infections in the human body?
What surgical procedure is performed to remove a clouded lens from the eye?
What type of patient care does a nurse provide when assisting a patient with open wounds or sores?
Which of the following is a standard credential for becoming a registered nurse in most countries?
choices: Nursing degree or diploma and licensure exam

questions.q: How do nurses typically assist physicians in identifying patient health problems?
choices: By conducting assessments and providing patient data

questions.q: What professional nursing organization in the United States sets standards for nursing practice?
Which healthcare setting is known as the primary location where a patient first seeks medical care?
What type of professional license is required for someone to work as a registered nurse?
choices: A state-issued nursing license or RN certification

questions.q: In healthcare delivery, which profession makes up the largest percentage of healthcare workers in most settings?
Which famous nurse wrote 'Notes on Nursing' in 1860?
Who founded the American Red Cross in 1881?
In what conflict did Florence Nightingale gain recognition for her nursing contributions?
What is the primary responsibility of a nurse before administering a blood transfusion?
choices: Warming the blood to room temperature
Verifying the correct blood type matches the patient
Obtaining written permission from the hospital administrator

questions.q: In which country is nursing regulated by the Nursing Council to ensure high professional standards?
What educational requirement is typically needed to enter a nursing program in Iran?
choices: Five years of hospital volunteer work
A secondary school diploma and passing entrance exams

questions.q: How do nurses assist physicians in diagnosing patient health problems?
choices: By conducting patient assessments and providing clinical data

questions.q: In advanced hemodynamic monitoring, what does a pulmonary artery catheter (Swan-Ganz catheter) primarily measure that helps clinicians assess left ventricular function?
Which nursing intervention is most appropriate for managing a patient experiencing septic shock related to the pathophysiology of distributive shock?
choices: Delayed antibiotic administration until culture results confirm infection
Restriction of all intravenous fluids to prevent pulmonary edema
Vasodilator therapy as the primary first-line intervention
Immediate fluid resuscitation and early broad-spectrum antibiotics within the first hour

questions.q: In the context of ARDS (Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome), what is the primary nursing role in managing mechanical ventilation settings to minimize ventilator-induced lung injury?
choices: Avoiding any sedation to allow spontaneous breathing efforts
Maintaining high positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP) above 20 cm H₂O universally
Monitoring lung-protective ventilation with low tidal volumes (6-8 mL/kg of predicted body weight) and plateau pressures
Maximizing tidal volumes to improve oxygenation regardless of pressure limits

questions.q: Which assessment finding would indicate the development of disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) in a critically ill patient?
choices: Decreased white blood cell count with elevated fibrinogen levels
Prolonged PT/INR, decreased platelet count, decreased fibrinogen, and elevated D-dimer
Normal platelet count with shortened activated partial thromboplastin time
Elevated hemoglobin with normal coagulation studies

questions.q: In managing a patient with thyroid storm, which nursing intervention directly addresses the pathophysiology of excessive thyroid hormone release?
choices: Administering propranolol for adrenergic symptom control and propylthiouracil (PTU) to inhibit thyroid hormone synthesis
Administering levothyroxine to increase thyroid hormone production
Restricting iodine intake to reduce thyroid function
Providing only cooling measures without pharmacological intervention

questions.q: What is the pathophysiological basis for administering sodium bicarbonate in the management of tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose?
choices: Bicarbonate directly neutralizes all TCA molecules in the bloodstream
It prevents the formation of toxic metabolites in the liver
Alkalinization increases TCA protein binding and renal excretion, reducing free drug toxicity
Alkalinization has no pharmacological effect but improves patient comfort

questions.q: In oncology nursing, what is the primary mechanism of action of checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapies such as nivolumab and pembrolizumab?
choices: Blocking PD-1/PD-L1 pathways to remove immune tolerance and enhance T-cell activation against tumor cells
Preventing angiogenesis by inhibiting VEGF signaling
Replacing deficient white blood cells through bone marrow transplantation
Directly killing cancer cells through cytotoxic chemotherapy

questions.q: Which nursing consideration is critical when managing a patient receiving intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) therapy for autoimmune conditions?
choices: Monitoring for volume overload, aseptic meningitis, acute kidney injury, and thrombotic events
Administering bolus doses as rapidly as possible to maximize efficacy
Ensuring the patient remains in a warm environment to prevent immunosuppression
Withholding all other medications to prevent drug interactions

questions.q: In nephrology nursing, what does the fractional excretion of sodium (FENa) primarily help distinguish in acute kidney injury assessment?
choices: Prerenal azotemia from intrinsic renal disease by measuring the proportion of filtered sodium that is excreted
The patient's hydration status based on urine osmolality alone
The presence of urinary tract infection

questions.q: Which electrocardiographic finding and corresponding nursing intervention is most appropriate for a patient with acute anterior wall ST-elevation myocardial infarction?
choices: Sinus tachycardia is the only finding needed to initiate emergency protocols
PR prolongation indicates the need for temporary pacemaker placement immediately
ST elevation in leads V1-V4 requires urgent reperfusion therapy via primary percutaneous coronary intervention or fibrinolysis
T wave inversion alone is sufficient to diagnose acute MI requiring immediate intervention

questions.q: In managing Guillain-Barré syndrome, why is close monitoring of forced vital capacity and negative inspiratory force essential in nursing care?
choices: To monitor pain levels in the patient's extremities
To detect ascending paralysis affecting respiratory muscles and determine the need for mechanical ventilation before respiratory failure occurs
Because these measurements directly cure the underlying autoimmune condition
Because they indicate the patient's readiness for immediate discharge

questions.q: Which nursing assessment finding would suggest the development of superior vena cava syndrome in an oncology patient?
choices: Lower extremity edema and deep vein thrombosis
Hypotension with tachycardia and decreased central venous pressure
Facial plethora, upper extremity edema, neck vein distension, and chest wall collateral veins

questions.q: In managing a patient with hyperkalemia secondary to chronic kidney disease, what is the mechanism of action of sodium polystyrene sulfonate (Kayexalate)?
choices: Enhancement of renal potassium filtration through loop diuretics
Ion exchange resin that trades sodium for potassium in the gastrointestinal tract, promoting fecal potassium excretion
Precipitation of potassium in the bloodstream for removal via dialysis
Direct inhibition of the sodium-potassium ATPase pump

questions.q: Which nursing intervention is most critical in the first 24 hours following a major thermal burn to prevent burn shock and organ failure?
choices: Fluid resuscitation using the Parkland formula (4 mL × %TBSA × body weight in kg) titrated to maintain urine output of 0.5 mL/kg/hr
Immediate application of topical antibiotics to all burned surfaces
Delayed fluid administration until laboratory values indicate hypovolemia
Restriction of all oral and intravenous fluids to prevent infection

questions.q: In the management of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), what is the rationale for insulin therapy only after serum potassium is confirmed to be adequate?
choices: High potassium levels enhance insulin effectiveness in DKA
Insulin drives potassium intracellularly, and administering it with hypokalemia risks fatal cardiac arrhythmias
Insulin therapy is contraindicated in all DKA cases regardless of potassium
Potassium levels have no relationship to insulin administration

questions.q: Which nursing assessment is essential for detecting early signs of necrotizing fasciitis in a postoperative surgical patient?
choices: Monitoring the patient's temperature for high fevers only
Checking the surgical incision for normal serous drainage only
Assessing wound appearance every 48 hours as part of routine care
Evaluating for rapidly advancing erythema, severe pain disproportionate to physical findings, skin crepitus, and systemic toxicity

questions.q: In managing a patient on continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT), what does the effluent flow rate directly determine?
choices: The patient's blood pressure and heart rate exclusively
The type of anticoagulation required in the CRRT circuit
The clearance of uremic waste products and fluid removal rate, requiring individualization based on patient acuity and metabolic demands

questions.q: Which pathophysiological complication of massive transfusion can result from rapid infusion of cold, citrate-containing blood products, and what is the nursing intervention?
choices: Coagulopathy from citrate chelation of ionized calcium and hypothermia; administer calcium chloride/gluconate and use blood warmers
Hyperkalemia exclusively; restrict potassium-containing medications only

questions.q: In obstetric nursing, what is the pathophysiological mechanism by which magnesium sulfate prevents seizures in severe preeclampsia?
choices: Stabilization of neuronal membranes and reduction of cerebral edema through blocking NMDA receptors and stabilizing vasomotor tone
Prevention of placental abruption through improved placental perfusion
Direct lowering of blood pressure to normal levels

questions.q: In the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation used in nursing assessment of acid-base balance, what does the ratio of HCO3- to dissolved CO2 primarily determine?
What is the primary physiological mechanism by which diuretics increase urine output, and how do nurses monitor for resulting electrolyte imbalances?
choices: By increasing glomerular filtration rate; monitoring serum potassium and sodium levels
By inhibiting angiotensin conversion; monitoring creatinine clearance only
By reducing antidiuretic hormone; monitoring blood glucose
By stimulating the sympathetic nervous system; monitoring heart rate variability

questions.q: In trauma nursing, what is the significance of the 'golden hour' concept, and which assessment takes priority in primary survey?
choices: Time window for potential reversal of shock; airway, breathing, circulation assessment
Time window for fluid resuscitation; abdominal ultrasound findings
Time window for surgical intervention; wound classification
Time window for optimal pain management; neurological assessment

questions.q: Which cytokine is primarily responsible for fever response in acute inflammation, and what nursing intervention addresses its physiological effects?
choices: Tumor necrosis factor-beta; application of heat therapy
Interleukin-1; administration of antipyretics and external cooling measures

questions.q: In oncology nursing, what is the purpose of calculating body surface area (BSA) rather than using weight alone for chemotherapy dosing?
choices: To eliminate the need for renal function testing
To account for variable drug metabolism and distribution based on body composition
To simplify nursing documentation and reduce calculation errors
To reduce medication costs and insurance claims

questions.q: What is the Braden Scale used to assess in nursing practice, and which score range indicates high risk?
choices: Pressure injury risk; scores of 18 or below

questions.q: In pediatric nursing, what is the primary reason for calculating medication doses using weight-based dosing (mg/kg) rather than fixed adult doses?
choices: To reduce healthcare costs for pediatric patients
To standardize nursing documentation across all age groups
To eliminate the need for parental consent during medication administration
To account for developmental differences in absorption, distribution, and elimination

questions.q: What is the primary function of the nursing process framework in clinical decision-making?
choices: To reduce documentation requirements and administrative burden
To provide a systematic, evidence-based approach to patient assessment, planning, intervention, and evaluation
To standardize all nursing interventions across different patient populations
To eliminate all subjective patient information from care planning

questions.q: In critical care nursing, what is the significance of the APACHE II score, and what does it predict?
choices: Severity of illness and predicted hospital mortality risk in intensive care patients
Infection risk and need for antibiotic prophylaxis
Patient mobility and discharge readiness; functional capacity

questions.q: In wound care nursing, what is the primary advantage of using negative pressure wound therapy (NPWT) in treating complex wounds?
choices: It eliminates the need for any antimicrobial agents or dressings
It prevents all bacterial colonization permanently without additional interventions
It reduces the need for pain management during dressing changes
It promotes granulation tissue formation and removes interstitial fluid to reduce edema

questions.q: What is the primary pathophysiological basis for hyperkalemia in acute kidney injury, and what is the nursing priority?
choices: Accelerated cellular breakdown; promoting protein synthesis
Increased aldosterone production; increasing sodium intake
Decreased potassium intake; encouraging potassium-rich foods
Impaired renal excretion leading to potassium accumulation; monitoring cardiac dysrhythmias and ECG changes

questions.q: In gerontological nursing, what is the primary mechanism of age-related changes in medication metabolism, and how does this affect nursing interventions?
choices: Enhanced gastrointestinal absorption; accelerating drug action
Increased plasma protein binding; improving drug distribution
Decreased renal function and hepatic metabolism; requiring dose adjustments and careful monitoring for toxicity
Increased hepatic metabolism; requiring higher drug doses

questions.q: What is the primary purpose of the Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) in nursing assessment, and what does a score of 8 or less indicate?
choices: Determines fall risk in hospitalized patients; indicates high fall precautions needed
Assesses pain severity; indicates moderate pain requiring intervention
Evaluates level of consciousness and neurological status; indicates severe impairment with need for airway protection
Measures depression risk in psychiatric patients; indicates suicidal ideation

questions.q: In palliative care nursing, what is the primary goal when managing opioid-induced constipation?
choices: Switching exclusively to non-pharmacological pain management methods
Implementing preventive bowel management protocols including stool softeners and stimulant laxatives
Discontinuing opioid therapy immediately to resolve constipation
Accepting constipation as an unavoidable side effect without intervention

questions.q: What is the primary principle of the Neuman Systems Model in nursing, and how does it guide nursing interventions?
choices: The client is viewed as a dynamic system responding to stressors, with nursing interventions at primary, secondary, and tertiary prevention levels
Nursing focuses only on eliminating disease symptoms through medication
Nursing interventions aim solely at acute crisis management
Nursing practice is standardized without individual client assessment

questions.q: In psychiatric nursing, what is the primary therapeutic mechanism of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), and what is an important nursing consideration during initial therapy?
choices: They increase dopamine; monitoring for hypertensive crisis immediately
They decrease acetylcholine; managing anticholinergic side effects only
They increase synaptic serotonin; monitoring for increased suicidality in early treatment, especially in young adults
They block norepinephrine; monitoring blood glucose levels continuously

questions.q: What is the significance of the Quick Sequential Organ Failure Assessment (qSOFA) score in sepsis screening, and what are its three components?
choices: Blood culture results, white blood cell count, and lactate level
Core body temperature, heart rate, and oxygen saturation
Altered consciousness, respiratory rate ≥22, and systolic blood pressure ≤100 mmHg
Procalcitonin level, fever, and hypotension only

questions.q: In maternal-neonatal nursing, what is the primary function of performing the Leopold maneuvers during prenatal assessment?
choices: To evaluate placental sufficiency through maternal vital signs
To assess for gestational diabetes complications
To measure amniotic fluid volume accurately
To determine fetal position, presentation, and engagement through abdominal palpation

questions.q: In the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation used in nursing assessment of arterial blood gases, what does the 'HCO3-' variable represent?
Which nursing intervention is most appropriate for a patient experiencing acute compartment syndrome to prevent permanent tissue damage?
In the context of fluid and electrolyte balance, what is the primary role of aldosterone that nurses must monitor when assessing sodium and potassium levels?
choices: Increasing sodium reabsorption and potassium excretion in the kidneys

questions.q: A nurse is caring for a patient receiving intravenous heparin therapy. What is the primary laboratory value that must be monitored to assess therapeutic efficacy and prevent complications?
In managing patients with acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), what is the rationale for utilizing lung-protective ventilation strategies with lower tidal volumes?
choices: To minimize ventilator-induced lung injury and reduce barotrauma
To increase oxygen delivery to peripheral tissues
To prevent bacterial colonization of airways
To eliminate the need for sedation

questions.q: Which nursing consideration is critical when administering vancomycin intravenously to minimize the risk of red man syndrome?
choices: Administering it as a rapid IV push
Giving it exclusively through central lines
Infusing the medication slowly over at least 60 minutes

questions.q: A patient presents with hyperkalemia of 6.8 mEq/L. What is the immediate priority intervention a nurse should anticipate to protect cardiac function?
choices: Administration of calcium gluconate or calcium chloride
Application of a cardiac monitor only

questions.q: In the nursing assessment of a patient with sepsis, what is the significance of lactate levels greater than 4 mmol/L?
choices: Indicator of tissue hypoperfusion and increased mortality risk

questions.q: When managing a patient with diabetic ketoacidosis, why do nurses prioritize careful fluid replacement and electrolyte monitoring alongside insulin therapy?
choices: To prevent hypokalemia and osmotic complications as glucose and ketones are cleared
To reduce the patient's thirst sensation
To eliminate the need for insulin

questions.q: A nurse caring for a post-operative patient notices signs of deep vein thrombosis (DVT). Which assessment finding would most strongly suggest venous insufficiency rather than acute thrombosis?
choices: Positive Homan's sign with calf tenderness
Chronic skin changes including hyperpigmentation and lipodermatosclerosis
Sudden onset of unilateral leg swelling

questions.q: In the nursing management of increased intracranial pressure (ICP), what is the physiological rationale for maintaining the head of bed elevation at 30 degrees?
choices: To promote cerebral venous drainage and decrease ICP through gravity
To increase blood flow to the brain
To reduce the need for osmotic therapy

questions.q: A patient on continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT) develops muscle weakness and fatigue. Which electrolyte abnormality is most likely causing these symptoms if phosphate losses are excessive?
In the context of negative pressure wound therapy, what is the primary mechanism by which subatmospheric pressure promotes wound healing?
choices: Stimulating granulation tissue formation and reducing interstitial edema
Eliminating all microorganisms from the wound bed
Increasing blood glucose at the wound site

questions.q: When administering a thrombolytic agent for acute ischemic stroke within the therapeutic window, which nursing assessment is critical to identify contraindications related to bleeding risk?
choices: Recent surgery, trauma, or anticoagulation therapy history

questions.q: A patient with severe hyponatremia (Na+ 115 mEq/L) develops altered mental status. Why must nurses avoid rapid sodium correction despite the neurological symptoms?
choices: Risk of osmotic demyelination syndrome causing permanent neurological damage
Rapid correction eliminates the need for ICU monitoring
Sodium is not necessary for brain function
It prevents the patient from regaining consciousness

questions.q: In caring for a patient with acute coronary syndrome receiving dual antiplatelet therapy, which laboratory marker best predicts myocardial necrosis within hours of symptom onset?
A nurse managing a patient on epidural anesthesia must monitor for epidural hematoma. Which clinical presentation would warrant immediate neurosurgical consultation?
choices: Progressive lower extremity weakness with bladder dysfunction
Mild lower back tenderness at catheter site
Expected numbness from the anesthetic agent

questions.q: When assessing a patient with suspected adrenal insufficiency, what is the clinical significance of a morning cortisol level combined with ACTH measurement in distinguishing primary from secondary adrenal failure?
choices: High ACTH with low cortisol indicates primary insufficiency; low ACTH with low cortisol indicates secondary insufficiency
Both conditions show identical ACTH and cortisol patterns
ACTH levels are irrelevant in adrenal assessment
Low ACTH always indicates primary disease

questions.q: In the nursing management of a patient with acute decompensated heart failure, what is the rationale for monitoring B-type natriuretic peptide (BNP) levels alongside diuretic therapy?
choices: To assess ventricular wall stress and guide therapy intensity and duration
To predict the patient's discharge date accurately
To eliminate the need for ACE inhibitors
To replace the need for echocardiography

questions.q: A patient receiving cisplatin chemotherapy develops symptoms of ototoxicity. Why does this adverse effect occur, and what preventive nursing measure is most evidence-based?
choices: Only genetic predisposition determines this complication
Ototoxicity is irreversible and cannot be prevented
Cisplatin causes cochlear damage; aggressive hydration and amifostine administration reduce risk

questions.q: In the CURB-65 severity assessment tool used by nurses to evaluate pneumonia risk, what does the 'U' stand for?
Which nursing intervention is the gold standard for preventing ventilator-associated pneumonia in mechanically ventilated patients?
In hemodynamic monitoring, what does a pulmonary artery wedge pressure (PAWP) reading above 18 mmHg typically indicate?
choices: Left ventricular dysfunction or pulmonary edema

questions.q: Which adverse effect is most commonly associated with prolonged use of central venous catheters in nursing care?
According to the Braden Scale, which of the following assessment categories has the strongest independent predictive value for pressure injury development?
In trans-esophageal echocardiography (TEE) nursing support, what is the primary contraindication that must be assessed before the procedure?
choices: Esophageal stricture or recent esophageal surgery

questions.q: Which electrolyte abnormality requires the most cautious correction rate by nurses to prevent central pontine myelinolysis?
In pharmacokinetics monitoring, what clinical phenomenon describes the nonlinear relationship between digoxin dose and serum concentration that nurses must account for?
Which scoring system do nurses use to quantify severity in acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) based on oxygenation parameters?
In oncology nursing, which chemotherapy agent requires nurses to monitor for cumulative cardiotoxicity with serial echocardiograms due to anthracycline-induced cardiomyopathy?
What is the pathophysiological mechanism behind nurse-monitored manifestations of superior vena cava syndrome including facial edema and upper extremity venous distention?
choices: Impaired venous return from compression or thrombosis of the SVC
Decreased cardiac output from right atrial obstruction
Pulmonary hypertension causing right heart failure

questions.q: What is the primary purpose of blood typing before administering a transfusion?
choices: To identify ABO and Rh compatibility between donor and recipient
To assess the patient's immune system function
To determine the patient's oxygen saturation level
To measure the hemoglobin concentration in the blood

questions.q: Which nursing intervention is most appropriate for a patient experiencing hypertension?
choices: Limiting all physical activity to reduce cardiac workload
Administering antihypertensive medications and promoting lifestyle modifications
Encouraging high sodium intake to maintain electrolyte balance
Increasing fluid intake to dilute blood volume

questions.q: What is the primary nursing responsibility in maintaining patient nutritional status?
choices: Documenting food preferences but not nutritional adequacy
Deciding the patient's diet without consulting healthcare providers
Assessing nutritional needs, monitoring intake, and coordinating with dietitians
Requiring all patients to consume standard hospital meals

questions.q: Which diagnostic tool allows nurses to assess arterial blood gas levels?
What does the Glasgow Coma Scale assess in nursing practice?
choices: Level of consciousness and neurological status

questions.q: In medication administration, what does the 'right time' principle require?
choices: Delaying medication until the patient requests it
Administering medication whenever convenient for staff
Giving medication at times that best suit the patient's schedule
Administering medication at prescribed intervals for therapeutic effectiveness

questions.q: Which nursing diagnosis framework is most commonly used in clinical practice?
choices: NANDA-I (North American Nursing Diagnosis Association International)

questions.q: What does 'patient advocacy' mean in the nursing profession?
choices: Avoiding involvement in patient care decisions
Agreeing with all physician orders without question
Prioritizing hospital policies over patient welfare
Speaking on behalf of patients' rights, needs, and preferences

questions.q: In wound care, what is the primary purpose of maintaining a moist wound environment?
choices: To promote faster healing, reduce scarring, and decrease pain
To dry out the wound as quickly as possible
To allow bacteria to multiply and cleanse the wound naturally
To prevent any new tissue formation

questions.q: What is the significance of obtaining informed consent before nursing procedures?
choices: It protects only the hospital from liability
It is a legal formality with no clinical importance
It is only necessary for major surgical interventions
It ensures the patient understands the procedure and voluntarily agrees to it

questions.q: Which nursing specialty focuses specifically on managing acute and chronic heart conditions?
What does the acronym 'SBAR' represent in nursing communication?
In the nursing process, what does the 'implementation' phase involve?
choices: Evaluating whether patient outcomes have been achieved
Determining what problems the patient might develop
Executing the planned nursing interventions and documenting care
Collecting patient health data and making diagnoses

questions.q: Which principle guides nurses to avoid harm and promote patient safety?
Which nursing theorist developed the concept of 'Environmental Theory' emphasizing the importance of clean air, light, and sanitation in patient recovery?
What is the primary purpose of the nursing assessment phase in the nursing process?
choices: To gather comprehensive patient data including health history and vital signs
To determine the most cost-effective treatment plan
To evaluate patient outcomes after intervention

questions.q: In cardiac nursing, what does the abbreviation 'ACLS' stand for?
Which nursing intervention is most appropriate for a patient experiencing orthostatic hypotension?
choices: Keeping the patient in a supine position at all times
Administering high-sodium foods to increase blood volume
Encouraging rapid position changes to promote circulation
Instructing the patient to change positions slowly and sit briefly before standing

questions.q: Which vital sign is considered abnormal if it exceeds 100 beats per minute at rest in an adult?
In infection control, what does 'Standard Precautions' require nurses to use for all patients regardless of diagnosis?
choices: Full hazmat suits for every patient encounter
Gloves, hand hygiene, and personal protective equipment as appropriate
Isolation gowns only when patients are symptomatic
Antibiotics to prevent all potential infections

questions.q: What is the primary role of a nurse during the informed consent process before surgery?
choices: To ensure the patient understands the procedure and has voluntarily agreed to treatment
To determine which anesthesia will be used

questions.q: Which assessment finding would indicate that a patient's peripheral circulation is compromised?
choices: Cool, pale extremities with delayed capillary refill
Warm, pink extremities with brisk capillary refill

questions.q: What is the recommended action if a nurse accidentally administers the wrong medication to a patient?
choices: Immediately notify the physician and document the incident in an incident report
Wait to see if adverse effects occur before reporting
Ask the patient not to mention it
Document it privately to avoid embarrassment

questions.q: Which nursing diagnosis would be most appropriate for a patient with limited mobility due to arthritis?
choices: Imbalanced Nutrition: Less Than Body Requirements

questions.q: What is the primary purpose of performing a hand-off or change-of-shift report in nursing?
choices: To entertain staff with patient stories
To blame the previous shift for any problems
To ensure continuity of care by communicating critical patient information to the next shift
To document all tasks completed during the shift

questions.q: In respiratory nursing, what does 'pursed-lip breathing' primarily help patients achieve?
choices: Slower exhalation and improved oxygenation in chronic obstructive pulmonary disease
Reduction of anxiety through meditative practices

questions.q: What is the term used in nursing to describe a patient's unwillingness or inability to follow medical advice?
Which nutrient is most critical for nurses to monitor in patients with chronic kidney disease?
What is the primary goal of therapeutic communication techniques used by nurses?
choices: To minimize time spent with patients
To establish trust and facilitate the patient's expression of concerns
To provide entertainment during hospital stays
To establish the nurse's authority over the patient

questions.q: In pain management, what does the 'pain ladder' or 'analgesic ladder' recommend for stepped medication escalation?
choices: Begin with non-opioid analgesics, then add adjuvants or opioids as pain increases
Avoid all pain medication unless absolutely necessary

questions.q: What is the significance of monitoring intake and output in hospitalized patients?
choices: It determines the patient's insurance coverage
It monitors fluid balance and helps detect kidney dysfunction or dehydration
It helps track patient costs for billing purposes
It ensures patients receive entertainment throughout the day

questions.q: Which nursing action is most appropriate when a patient expresses suicidal thoughts?
choices: Discuss it only with family members
Leave them alone to think through their feelings
Take the statement seriously, ensure safety, and immediately notify the healthcare team
Assure them it is just a phase and they will feel better soon

questions.q: In wound care nursing, what is the primary purpose of sterile dressing changes?
choices: To make wounds more visible to visitors
To reduce the frequency of patient assessment
To apply cosmetic coverings to surgical sites
To prevent infection and promote healing by maintaining a clean environment

questions.q: Which nursing assessment technique involves listening to internal body sounds using a stethoscope?
What is the primary purpose of the nursing diagnosis step in the nursing process?
choices: To identify patient health problems based on collected assessment data
To discharge the patient from the hospital
To determine the patient's insurance coverage
To prescribe medications for the patient

questions.q: Which organ system is primarily responsible for transporting oxygen and nutrients throughout the body?
What does the abbreviation 'NPO' mean in nursing care instructions?
Which of the following is a responsibility of nurses during patient discharge planning?
choices: Ensuring the patient has follow-up appointments and understands medication instructions

questions.q: Which nursing specialty focuses on caring for patients in critical care units with life-threatening conditions?
Which infection control practice is most critical before performing any patient care procedure?
In medication administration, what does the 'right dose' principle refer to?
choices: The patient's preference for medication timing
Giving extra medication for severe symptoms
Administering the correct amount of medication as prescribed

questions.q: Which nursing role involves providing care to patients in their homes?
What is the primary purpose of a nursing care plan?
choices: To entertain the patient during hospitalization
To outline individualized interventions and expected patient outcomes

questions.q: Which communication technique involves summarizing what a patient has said to ensure understanding?
What is the abbreviation 'IV' in nursing commonly used to indicate?
Which of the following is a key principle of ethical nursing practice?
choices: Making all medical decisions without patient input
Prioritizing hospital profits over patient welfare
Respecting patient autonomy and informed consent

questions.q: What is the role of documentation in nursing practice?
choices: To prevent patients from accessing their information
To create busy work for nurses
To provide a legal record of patient care and communication between healthcare providers

questions.q: Which nursing assessment finding would warrant immediate intervention for a patient at risk of falling?
Which nursing theorist developed the concept of 'caring' as the central focus of nursing practice?
In the nursing process, what is the second step after assessment?
What is the normal range for adult human body temperature in Fahrenheit?
Which of the following is a primary responsibility of nurses when administering intravenous medications?
choices: Storing medications in patient rooms for easy access
Verifying the medication order and monitoring for adverse reactions

questions.q: What does HIPAA stand for in the context of nursing and healthcare?
choices: Health Insurance Privacy and Accountability Act
Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act
Health Information Privacy and Accessibility Act
Hospital Information Protection and Access Act

questions.q: What is the primary purpose of a patient's advance directive in nursing care?
choices: To grant nurses authority to make all medical decisions
To document the patient's wishes regarding end-of-life medical treatment
To limit the types of medications a patient can receive

questions.q: In infection control, what is the main goal of standard precautions?
choices: To reduce transmission of pathogens from all patients and environments
To treat only patients with diagnosed infections
To eliminate the need for handwashing between patients
To apply only to healthcare workers with respiratory symptoms

questions.q: Which nursing intervention is most appropriate for a patient experiencing acute anxiety?
choices: Immediately administering sedative medications without assessment
Using a calm voice, providing reassurance, and staying with the patient
Leaving the patient alone to calm down independently
Asking the patient to distract themselves with strenuous exercise
